      62              : 
      63              : /*
      64              :  * All plpgsql function executions within a single transaction share the same
      65              :  * executor EState for evaluating "simple" expressions.  Each function call
      66              :  * creates its own "eval_econtext" ExprContext within this estate for
      67              :  * per-evaluation workspace.  eval_econtext is freed at normal function exit,
      68              :  * and the EState is freed at transaction end (in case of error, we assume
      69              :  * that the abort mechanisms clean it all up).  Furthermore, any exception
      70              :  * block within a function has to have its own eval_econtext separate from
      71              :  * the containing function's, so that we can clean up ExprContext callbacks
      72              :  * properly at subtransaction exit.  We maintain a stack that tracks the
      73              :  * individual econtexts so that we can clean up correctly at subxact exit.
      74              :  *
      75              :  * This arrangement is a bit tedious to maintain, but it's worth the trouble
      76              :  * so that we don't have to re-prepare simple expressions on each trip through
      77              :  * a function.  (We assume the case to optimize is many repetitions of a
      78              :  * function within a transaction.)
      79              :  *
      80              :  * However, there's no value in trying to amortize simple expression setup
      81              :  * across multiple executions of a DO block (inline code block), since there
      82              :  * can never be any.  If we use the shared EState for a DO block, the expr
      83              :  * state trees are effectively leaked till end of transaction, and that can
      84              :  * add up if the user keeps on submitting DO blocks.  Therefore, each DO block
      85              :  * has its own simple-expression EState, which is cleaned up at exit from
      86              :  * plpgsql_inline_handler().  DO blocks still use the simple_econtext_stack,
      87              :  * though, so that subxact abort cleanup does the right thing.
      88              :  *
      89              :  * (However, if a DO block executes COMMIT or ROLLBACK, then exec_stmt_commit
      90              :  * or exec_stmt_rollback will unlink it from the DO's simple-expression EState
      91              :  * and create a new shared EState that will be used thenceforth.  The original
      92              :  * EState will be cleaned up when we get back to plpgsql_inline_handler.  This
      93              :  * is a bit ugly, but it isn't worth doing better, since scenarios like this
      94              :  * can't result in indefinite accumulation of state trees.)
      95              :  */
      96              : typedef struct SimpleEcontextStackEntry
      97              : {
      98              :         ExprContext *stack_econtext;    /* a stacked econtext */
      99              :         SubTransactionId xact_subxid;   /* ID for current subxact */
     100              :         struct SimpleEcontextStackEntry *next;  /* next stack entry up */
     101              : } SimpleEcontextStackEntry;
     102              : 
     103              : static EState *shared_simple_eval_estate = NULL;
     104              : static SimpleEcontextStackEntry *simple_econtext_stack = NULL;
     105              : 
     106              : /*
     107              :  * In addition to the shared simple-eval EState, we have a shared resource
     108              :  * owner that holds refcounts on the CachedPlans for any "simple" expressions
     109              :  * we have evaluated in the current transaction.  This allows us to avoid
     110              :  * continually grabbing and releasing a plan refcount when a simple expression
     111              :  * is used over and over.  (DO blocks use their own resowner, in exactly the
     112              :  * same way described above for shared_simple_eval_estate.)
     113              :  */
     114              : static ResourceOwner shared_simple_eval_resowner = NULL;
     115              : 
     116              : /*
     117              :  * Memory management within a plpgsql function generally works with three
     118              :  * contexts:
     119              :  *
     120              :  * 1. Function-call-lifespan data, such as variable values, is kept in the
     121              :  * "main" context, a/k/a the "SPI Proc" context established by SPI_connect().
     122              :  * This is usually the CurrentMemoryContext while running code in this module
     123              :  * (which is not good, because careless coding can easily cause
     124              :  * function-lifespan memory leaks, but we live with it for now).
     125              :  *
     126              :  * 2. Some statement-execution routines need statement-lifespan workspace.
     127              :  * A suitable context is created on-demand by get_stmt_mcontext(), and must
     128              :  * be reset at the end of the requesting routine.  Error recovery will clean
     129              :  * it up automatically.  Nested statements requiring statement-lifespan
     130              :  * workspace will result in a stack of such contexts, see push_stmt_mcontext().
     131              :  *
     132              :  * 3. We use the eval_econtext's per-tuple memory context for expression
     133              :  * evaluation, and as a general-purpose workspace for short-lived allocations.
     134              :  * Such allocations usually aren't explicitly freed, but are left to be
     135              :  * cleaned up by a context reset, typically done by exec_eval_cleanup().
     136              :  *
     137              :  * These macros are for use in making short-lived allocations:
     138              :  */
     139              : #define get_eval_mcontext(estate) \
     140              :         ((estate)->eval_econtext->ecxt_per_tuple_memory)
     141              : #define eval_mcontext_alloc(estate, sz) \
     142              :         MemoryContextAlloc(get_eval_mcontext(estate), sz)
     143              : #define eval_mcontext_alloc0(estate, sz) \
     144              :         MemoryContextAllocZero(get_eval_mcontext(estate), sz)
     145              : 
     146              : /*
     147              :  * We use two session-wide hash tables for caching cast information.
     148              :  *
     149              :  * cast_expr_hash entries (of type plpgsql_CastExprHashEntry) hold compiled
     150              :  * expression trees for casts.  These survive for the life of the session and
     151              :  * are shared across all PL/pgSQL functions and DO blocks.  At some point it
     152              :  * might be worth invalidating them after pg_cast changes, but for the moment
     153              :  * we don't bother.
     154              :  *
     155              :  * There is a separate hash table shared_cast_hash (with entries of type
     156              :  * plpgsql_CastHashEntry) containing evaluation state trees for these
     157              :  * expressions, which are managed in the same way as simple expressions
     158              :  * (i.e., we assume cast expressions are always simple).
     159              :  *
     160              :  * As with simple expressions, DO blocks don't use the shared_cast_hash table
     161              :  * but must have their own evaluation state trees.  This isn't ideal, but we
     162              :  * don't want to deal with multiple simple_eval_estates within a DO block.
     163              :  */
     164              : typedef struct                                  /* lookup key for cast info */
     165              : {
     166              :         /* NB: we assume this struct contains no padding bytes */
     167              :         Oid                     srctype;                /* source type for cast */
     168              :         Oid                     dsttype;                /* destination type for cast */
     169              :         int32           srctypmod;              /* source typmod for cast */
     170              :         int32           dsttypmod;              /* destination typmod for cast */
     171              : } plpgsql_CastHashKey;
     172              : 
     173              : typedef struct                                  /* cast_expr_hash table entry */
     174              : {
     175              :         plpgsql_CastHashKey key;        /* hash key --- MUST BE FIRST */
     176              :         Expr       *cast_expr;          /* cast expression, or NULL if no-op cast */
     177              :         CachedExpression *cast_cexpr;   /* cached expression backing the above */
     178              : } plpgsql_CastExprHashEntry;
     179              : 
     180              : typedef struct                                  /* cast_hash table entry */
     181              : {
     182              :         plpgsql_CastHashKey key;        /* hash key --- MUST BE FIRST */
     183              :         plpgsql_CastExprHashEntry *cast_centry; /* link to matching expr entry */
     184              :         /* ExprState is valid only when cast_lxid matches current LXID */
     185              :         ExprState  *cast_exprstate; /* expression's eval tree */
     186              :         bool            cast_in_use;    /* true while we're executing eval tree */
     187              :         LocalTransactionId cast_lxid;
     188              : } plpgsql_CastHashEntry;
     189              : 
     190              : static HTAB *cast_expr_hash = NULL;
     191              : static HTAB *shared_cast_hash = NULL;
     192              : 
     193              : /*
     194              :  * LOOP_RC_PROCESSING encapsulates common logic for looping statements to
     195              :  * handle return/exit/continue result codes from the loop body statement(s).
     196              :  * It's meant to be used like this:
     197              :  *
     198              :  *              int rc = PLPGSQL_RC_OK;
     199              :  *              for (...)
     200              :  *              {
     201              :  *                      ...
     202              :  *                      rc = exec_stmts(estate, stmt->body);
     203              :  *                      LOOP_RC_PROCESSING(stmt->label, break);
     204              :  *                      ...
     205              :  *              }
     206              :  *              return rc;
     207              :  *
     208              :  * If execution of the loop should terminate, LOOP_RC_PROCESSING will execute
     209              :  * "exit_action" (typically a "break" or "goto"), after updating "rc" to the
     210              :  * value the current statement should return.  If execution should continue,
     211              :  * LOOP_RC_PROCESSING will do nothing except reset "rc" to PLPGSQL_RC_OK.
     212              :  *
     213              :  * estate and rc are implicit arguments to the macro.
     214              :  * estate->exitlabel is examined and possibly updated.
     215              :  */
     216              : #define LOOP_RC_PROCESSING(looplabel, exit_action) \
     217              :         if (rc == PLPGSQL_RC_RETURN) \
     218              :         { \
     219              :                 /* RETURN, so propagate RC_RETURN out */ \
     220              :                 exit_action; \
     221              :         } \
     222              :         else if (rc == PLPGSQL_RC_EXIT) \
     223              :         { \
     224              :                 if (estate->exitlabel == NULL) \
     225              :                 { \
     226              :                         /* unlabeled EXIT terminates this loop */ \
     227              :                         rc = PLPGSQL_RC_OK; \
     228              :                         exit_action; \
     229              :                 } \
     230              :                 else if ((looplabel) != NULL && \
     231              :                                  strcmp(looplabel, estate->exitlabel) == 0) \
     232              :                 { \
     233              :                         /* labeled EXIT matching this loop, so terminate loop */ \
     234              :                         estate->exitlabel = NULL; \
     235              :                         rc = PLPGSQL_RC_OK; \
     236              :                         exit_action; \
     237              :                 } \
     238              :                 else \
     239              :                 { \
     240              :                         /* non-matching labeled EXIT, propagate RC_EXIT out */ \
     241              :                         exit_action; \
     242              :                 } \
     243              :         } \
     244              :         else if (rc == PLPGSQL_RC_CONTINUE) \
     245              :         { \
     246              :                 if (estate->exitlabel == NULL) \
     247              :                 { \
     248              :                         /* unlabeled CONTINUE matches this loop, so continue in loop */ \
     249              :                         rc = PLPGSQL_RC_OK; \
     250              :                 } \
     251              :                 else if ((looplabel) != NULL && \
     252              :                                  strcmp(looplabel, estate->exitlabel) == 0) \
     253              :                 { \
     254              :                         /* labeled CONTINUE matching this loop, so continue in loop */ \
     255              :                         estate->exitlabel = NULL; \
     256              :                         rc = PLPGSQL_RC_OK; \
     257              :                 } \
     258              :                 else \
     259              :                 { \
     260              :                         /* non-matching labeled CONTINUE, propagate RC_CONTINUE out */ \
     261              :                         exit_action; \
     262              :                 } \
     263              :         } \
     264              :         else \
     265              :                 Assert(rc == PLPGSQL_RC_OK)
     266              :

     467              : /* ----------
     468              :  * plpgsql_exec_function        Called by the call handler for

    5457              : /*

    5930              : /* ----------
    5931              :  * exec_run_select                      Execute a select query
    5932              :  *
    5933              :  * Note: passing maxtuples different from 0 ("return all tuples") is
    5934              :  * deprecated because it will prevent parallel execution of the query.
    5935              :  * However, we retain the parameter in case we need it someday.

    5937              :  */

    5941              : {
    5942              :         ParamListInfo paramLI;
    5943              :         int                     rc;
    5944              : 
    5945              :         /*
    5946              :          * On the first call for this expression generate the plan.
    5947              :          *

    6017            0 :         uint64          previous_id = INVALID_TUPLEDESC_IDENTIFIER;

    6019              :         uint64          n;
    6020              : 
    6021              :         /* Fetch loop variable's datum entry */
    6022            0 :         var = (PLpgSQL_variable *) estate->datums[stmt->var->dno];
    6023              : 
    6024              :         /*
    6025              :          * Make sure the portal doesn't get closed by the user statements we

    6027              :          */
    6028            0 :         PinPortal(portal);

    6030              :         /*

    6033              :          * create, if we prefetch toasted data and then the user commits the
    6034              :          * transaction, the toast references could turn into dangling pointers.

    6048            0 :         n = SPI_processed;
    6049              :

    6092            0 :                                                                                           true, !estate->atomic);

    6111            0 :                                                 tupdescs_match =
    6112            0 :                                                         (rec->rectypeid == RECORDOID ||
    6113            0 :                                                          rec->rectypeid == tuptab->tupdesc->tdtypeid ||

    6164              : /* ----------
    6165              :  * exec_eval_simple_expr -              Evaluate a simple expression returning

    6411              : 
    6412              : /*
    6413              :  * Create a ParamListInfo to pass to SPI
    6414              :  *
    6415              :  * We use a single ParamListInfo struct for all SPI calls made from this
    6416              :  * estate; it contains no per-param data, just hook functions, so it's
    6417              :  * effectively read-only for SPI.
    6418              :  *
    6419              :  * An exception from pure read-only-ness is that the parserSetupArg points
    6420              :  * to the specific PLpgSQL_expr being evaluated.  This is not an issue for
    6421              :  * statement-level callers, but lower-level callers must save and restore
    6422              :  * estate->paramLI->parserSetupArg just in case there's an active evaluation
    6423              :  * at an outer call level.  (A plausible alternative design would be to
    6424              :  * create a ParamListInfo struct for each PLpgSQL_expr, but for the moment
    6425              :  * that seems like a waste of memory.)
    6426              :  */
    6427              : static ParamListInfo
    6428            0 : setup_param_list(PLpgSQL_execstate *estate, PLpgSQL_expr *expr)
    6429              : {
    6430              :         ParamListInfo paramLI;
    6431              : 
    6432              :         /*
    6433              :          * We must have created the SPIPlan already (hence, query text has been
    6434              :          * parsed/analyzed at least once); else we cannot rely on expr->paramnos.
    6435              :          */
    6436              :         Assert(expr->plan != NULL);
    6437              : 
    6438              :         /*

    6475              : /*

    6909              : 
    6910              : /*
    6911              :  * exec_move_row                        Move one tuple's values into a record or row
    6912              :  *
    6913              :  * tup and tupdesc may both be NULL if we're just assigning an indeterminate
    6914              :  * composite NULL to the target.  Alternatively, can have tup be NULL and
    6915              :  * tupdesc not NULL, in which case we assign a row of NULLs to the target.
    6916              :  *
    6917              :  * Since this uses the mcontext for workspace, caller should eventually call
    6918              :  * exec_eval_cleanup to prevent long-term memory leaks.
    6919              :  */

    7847              : /* ----------
    7848              :  * convert_value_to_string                      Convert a non-null Datum to C string
    7849              :  *
    7850              :  * Note: the result is in the estate's eval_mcontext, and will be cleared
    7851              :  * by the next exec_eval_cleanup() call.  The invoked output function might
    7852              :  * leave additional cruft there as well, so just pfree'ing the result string
    7853              :  * would not be enough to avoid memory leaks if we did not do it like this.
    7854              :  * In most usages the Datum being passed in is also in that context (if
    7855              :  * pass-by-reference) and so an exec_eval_cleanup() call is needed anyway.
    7856              :  *
    7857              :  * Note: not caching the conversion function lookup is bad for performance.

    7875            0 :         return result;

    7878              : /* ----------

    8118              : /* ----------
    8119              :  * exec_simple_check_plan -             Check if a plan is simple enough to

    8121              :  *                                                              of SPI.

    8136              :         /*
    8137              :          * Initialize to "not simple".

    8153              : 
    8154              :         /*

    8158              :          */

    8163              :         /* Can't fail, because we checked for a single CachedPlanSource above */

    8191              : /*
    8192              :  * exec_is_simple_query - precheck a query tree to see if it might be simple

    8267              :  * exec_save_simple_expr --- extract simple expression from CachedPlan

    8347              : /*
    8348              :  * exec_check_rw_parameter --- can we pass expanded object as read/write param?
    8349              :  *
    8350              :  * If we have an assignment like "x := array_append(x, foo)" in which the
    8351              :  * top-level function is trusted not to corrupt its argument in case of an
    8352              :  * error, then when x has an expanded object as value, it is safe to pass the
    8353              :  * value as a read/write pointer and let the function modify the value
    8354              :  * in-place.
    8355              :  *
    8356              :  * This function checks for a safe expression, and sets expr->rwparam to the
    8357              :  * dno of the target variable (x) if safe, or -1 if not safe.
    8358              :  */

    8360            0 : exec_check_rw_parameter(PLpgSQL_expr *expr, int target_dno)
    8361              : {
    8362              :         Oid                     funcid;
    8363              :         List       *fargs;
    8364              :         ListCell   *lc;
    8365              : 
    8366              :         /* Assume unsafe */
